title: Annex — Pathfinding: routing soldiers over terrain that moves
meta.desc: A technical annex: how Tower Duel's soldiers find their way to the enemy tower across destructible terrain riddled with works — a graph of surfaces, an A*, and the third player's oscilloscope that already IS the visualisation of that graph.
hero.title: Pathfinding: the graph you already see
hero.lead: How soldiers find their way to the enemy tower across destructible terrain riddled with works — and why the third player's oscilloscope is nothing but that navigation graph, shown as a HUD.
card.title: Pathfinding
card.desc: How soldiers find their way across destructible terrain riddled with structures — a graph of surfaces, an A*, and the third player's oscilloscope that already visualises it.
s0.lead: On their controller, the third player — the Intendant — has a little oscilloscope scanning the terrain. It isn't decoration: it is literally the soldiers' pathfinding graph, coloured segment by segment.
s0.p1: Each terrain segment is tinted by its slope-passability. Violet = passable both ways. Blue = climbable only left→right. Red = only right→left. Grey = a wall, impassable. The threshold, CS = 1.35, is exactly the engine's climbSlope: the visual translation of the rule “rise ≤ SU”.
s0.sim.title: The third player's oscilloscope
s0.sim.hint: The same render as on the controller: violet = both ways, blue = climb →, red = climb ←, grey = wall. The pale dots at the edges are towers; the coloured ones, soldiers.
s0.p2: In other words: the developers' debug tool (“Path debug”) has become a gameplay aid. The Intendant who wants to open a road for an army reads their scope to spot the red walls they must flatten or bridge. This annex unpacks what's behind that picture.
s0.callout.nerd: For the curious. The HUD does not recompute a real path every frame: it colours the local edges (the slope from one segment to the next). That's a sufficient, legible approximation. The real pathfinder runs server-side on the full graph — the subject of the next sections.
s1.p1: Tower Duel's terrain is a 1D heightfield: a single ground height per abscissa x. As long as that holds, following the slope is enough. But the Intendant places Lemmings-style works — bridges and stairs — that float above the ground. So at a single abscissa there can be several walkable surfaces: the ground, and one or more planks above it.
s1.p2: A greedy local follow (“walk, and if it's too steep, hug the obstacle”) fails the moment you must step onto a stair that starts far from a wall: the soldier, glued to the ground, can't “see” that ten metres back a ramp would let it clear the cliff. You need a global view of the terrain — a graph, and a search over it.
s1.callout.warn: Why not just “climb if you can”? Because a useful work often sits upstream of the obstacle it bypasses. A local algorithm never backtracks to fetch a bridge; a global pathfinder does. That's the whole point of a graph.
s2.p1: We sample the terrain into columns spaced STEP = 8 px apart. At each column we create not one node but one node per walkable surface: the ground, plus every plank passing above it. That's exactly what surfacesAt(x) returns:
s2.code.c1: // all walkable surfaces at abscissa x
s2.code.c2: // the ground is always a surface
s2.code.c3: // + every plank above (or flush with) the ground
s2.p2: A node is therefore the pair (column, surface). An edge links two adjacent columns when the step between their surfaces is passable. The diagram shows both storeys: the ground (violet) and a plank laid over a dip (blue) that creates a second row of nodes.
s2.fig.cap: Each abscissa can carry several nodes. A plank adds a surface above the ground; passable edges (violet) weave the graph, walls (red) cut it.
s2.callout.tip: Takeaway. No 2D grid, no tiling of space: the graph is sparse. It exists only where there's a surface to walk on — the ground and the planks. That's what keeps it small and fast.
s3.h2: The algorithm: an A* / Dijkstra over surfaces
s3.p1: The soldier seeks a path from its position to the abscissa of the enemy tower. We run a shortest-path search (Dijkstra, which becomes A* with a good heuristic). The core is the edge rule and the cost:
s3.code.c1: // climbing more than one SU step → impassable
s3.code.c2: // dropping costs (fall penalty); climbing a little costs too
s3.code.c3: // relax the edge if this path is better
s3.p2: A climb of more than SU (≈ one step) is forbidden: it's a wall. A drop is always allowed, but a big fall is penalised in the cost (the soldier gets hurt). We then reconstruct the sequence of waypoints by walking back through predecessors. And if no path reaches the enemy tower? The soldier deserts — it doesn't wander forever.
s3.p3: Two optional terms are added to the cost to decongest routes. First, the local soldier density (density[i2] × congW): a per-column occupancy field, rebuilt once per tick, counting every body — both camps and the horde, because crowding is physical, not a matter of allegiance. Then a per-soldier seeded jitter (hash(jseed, column)), drawn at its birth. The result: a horde fans out across the available routes instead of all queuing at the same bridge. Since both terms are strictly positive, they never remove an edge: feasibility is untouched, only the chosen route changes. The crossing test (crossPath) calls findPath without these options — it stays a pure A\*.
s3.callout.nerd: For the curious. This same pathfinder arbitrates the Intendant's score: at the end of a round, crossPath() tests whether a horde can cross to the ruin. No more false “impassable”: the very function that routes soldiers validates the crossing. The Intendant scores because they actually opened the road.
s4.h2: Following: stick to the real surface
s4.p1: Waypoints give a guideline, not a trajectory to glide along. At each step the soldier advances horizontally along the current segment, computes the planned height on that segment, then snaps to the real surface closest to that height. No soldier floating above the ground:
s4.code.c1: // planned height on the current segment
s4.code.c3: // the soldier follows the terrain, not the ideal line
s4.p2: This snapping naturally handles climbing and descending (and accumulates fall damage if the drop is harsh). Because path and follow are decoupled, a soldier can backtrack: if a replan hands it a better waypoint behind, it takes it without getting stuck.
s4.callout.tip: Takeaway. The pathfinder decides where to go; the follow decides how to plant a foot. Splitting the two makes movement robust: the terrain can change under the soldier's feet without breaking its walk, because height is always re-read off the current terrain.
s5.p1: The terrain shifts constantly: shell craters, bridges and stairs placed, earth dug or filled. A path computed a second ago may now be wrong. The fix is a simple version counter, navVer, incremented on every edit of terrain or structures. Each soldier remembers the navVer of its last computation and replans as soon as it changes:
s5.code.c1: // recompute if: no path, terrain changed, end of path,
s5.code.c2: // or the periodic ~0.7 s replan is due
s5.code.c3: // a fresh A* from the current position
s5.p2: navVer catches the discrete events (a crater, a plank). The periodic (~0.7 s) replan additionally catches continuous edits — digging or flattening frame by frame, which would otherwise bump navVer every tick. Together they keep paths fresh without recomputing everyone every frame.
s5.callout.nerd: For the curious. Replanning is not synced across soldiers: each one's s.rep timer starts at its birth, so recomputations spread out over time (staggering — see next section). Nobody pays a big spike on the same tick.
s6.p1: The graph's size is on the order of N columns × surfaces per column. Since there's almost never more than one or two surfaces per abscissa, it stays small. The naïve Dijkstra scans the queue to find the minimum at each step — an O(V²) cost, perfectly fine for this node count and a handful of soldiers.
s6.fig.stagNote: never all on the same tick 🚀
s6.fig.cap: A single path's raw cost is O(V²); what holds the load is recomputing each soldier only ~every 0.7 s, and out of phase.
s6.callout.tip: If one day there are many units. Two classic optimisations wait in the drawer: replace the linear queue with a binary heap (priority queue, Dijkstra in O(E log V)), and share a cache of paths between soldiers of the same camp aiming at the same tower. Premature today: the sim is trivially cheap at the current scale.
s7.h2: The debug tool: seeing the paths
s7.p1: The visual aid exists on both sides of the mirror. On the development side, the lab's “Path debug” button overlays on the terrain:
s7.li1: the valid paths computed by the real pathfinder, in green (left→right) and blue (right→left), with their waypoints;
s7.li2: the terrain's impassable slopes, in red (where the step exceeds climbSlope);
s7.li3: a waypoint count and an “OK / NONE” status for each direction — the crossing test, live.
s7.callout.nerd: The key angle. This debug tool is exactly what we ported into the third player's HUD (the opening section). The green/blue/red of “Path debug” and the violet/blue/red/grey of the oscilloscope are the same information, at two levels of aggregation: full paths for the dev, per-segment passability for the player.
s8.p1: The model is deliberately 1D + surfaces, not true 2D. That fixes its limits — by choice:
s8.li1: No covered tunnels. Digging horizontally opens a foot-level corridor (basher), digging down sinks a shaft (digger), but there's no “2D solidity” with a ceiling. Explicitly deferred.
s8.li2: Buried structures excluded. Construction stops if it would pass under the terrain — no ghost bridge buried that would pollute the graph.
s8.li3: Future ideas. An explicit A* heuristic (distance to the tower) to prune the search; a per-camp shared path cache. (Decongestion — cost ∝ soldier density + seeded jitter — is already in place: see §3.)
s8.callout.warn: A choice, not an oversight. Staying 1D + surfaces keeps the graph tiny, the follow robust, and replanning nearly free. True 2D would bring covered galleries — at the price of a much heavier graph and physics. To revisit only if the game demands it.
s9.callout.try: Your turn! The dedicated lab uses the real engine functions (surfacesAt, findPath). Place a plank, show the nodes and the graph, watch the A* path draw itself — then dig a crater and see navVer increment and the path recompute.
s9.p1: It's the same playground as the chapters, but centred on pathfinding: editable terrain, bridge/stair placement, graph and path visualisation, and the third player's oscilloscope up top to close the loop.
